summary: Enables more students to undertake recognised experiential and interdisciplinary learning beyond the boundaries of their course through shared institutional pathways that create space within their studies without requiring programmes to develop bespoke provision.
detailSummary:
Near-curriculum describes learning beyond the core disciplinary course that is formally docked with a student’s degree through credit or official institutional recognition. It creates legitimate space within the study pathway for students to work across disciplines, engage with external audiences and integrate meaningful experience into their education. Attaching credit or recognition signals that these experiences matter and improves access for students who have less capacity to undertake additional extracurricular activity.

King’s will establish shared academic, regulatory and administrative structures that programmes can adopt without creating bespoke provision or redesigning their disciplinary core. These will include the smallest viable suite of adaptable optional 15-credit modules, supporting interdisciplinary and vertical projects, work with external audiences, cross-disciplinary themes such as entrepreneurship, and a reflective capstone route through which experiential learning can receive credit or formal recognition. The work will explore practical and creative approaches to scheduling and timetabling, recognising the need for new provision to fit as smoothly as possible within the wider student experience.

The shared architecture will also make sandwich years easier for programmes to offer and students to access, including through transdisciplinary models. Integrating sandwich-year provision into King’s overarching curriculum and regulatory frameworks will allow common approval, administration and provisioning requirements to be addressed centrally, rather than requiring each programme to build and sustain its own route. This will create a broad institutional pathway while allowing programmes with legitimate academic or practical reasons to agree a variation.

The deliverable will also establish the governance and assurance routes required by other elements of King’s Edge, including the skills framework and associated microcredentials. Their substantive propositions will remain with the relevant deliverables, while 2.2.3 ensures that they can be governed, assured and formally recognised. Deliverable 2.2.2 will provide the shared partnership and operational infrastructure; 2.2.3 will own the academic, regulatory and administrative pathways through which learning becomes formal provision.

caseForChange.problem:
University has traditionally given students space to explore who they might become. Through their course and the wider university experience, students test possible futures, build confidence, develop relationships and gain experience. For some, these ambitions align closely with their discipline. For others, the future they begin to create sits alongside, crosses or departs substantially from the subject they study.

The graduate premium therefore extends beyond the learning outcomes of a course. It also emerges through the choices students make, the contexts they encounter and the opportunities they have to apply and extend their learning. Universities have relied heavily on extracurricular activity to carry this wider development, requiring students to find additional time around their formal studies and creating particular barriers for those with work, caring responsibilities or other demands.

Opportunities that cross disciplines, programmes or faculties are also difficult to create from a single academic base. Higher education lacks a scalable space between the course and extracurricular participation where students can pursue wider ambitions with dedicated time, credit or official recognition.
caseForChange.opportunity:
King’s can establish near-curriculum learning as a recognised part of the student journey, giving students space within their formal degree to pursue learning aligned with their purposes and future ambitions. Credit and official recognition can make interdisciplinary, experiential and externally connected opportunities more accessible, while allowing the disciplinary course to remain the organising core of their education.

A deliberately small number of shared module shells, recognition routes and regulatory frameworks can support a wide range of activity without requiring individual programmes to create bespoke provision. Common academic and administrative structures can reduce duplication, make successful models easier to reuse and provide a simpler basis for provision across programmes and faculties. The work can also explore creative approaches to scheduling and positioning these opportunities within the wider student experience.

This architecture can support interdisciplinary and vertical projects, reflective recognition of experiential learning, strategically aligned cross-disciplinary options and broader access to sandwich years. It can also provide the governance and assurance routes needed for the skills framework and associated microcredentials, supported by the partnership and operational infrastructure developed through 2.2.2.
caseForChange.whyNow:
Students are placing increasing weight on the wider value of university and the experiences, relationships and capabilities that support their future ambitions. At the same time, many have less discretionary time to pursue development outside their formal studies. King’s Edge therefore needs routes through which wider learning can become a recognised and accessible part of the degree.

Current work on the Curriculum Framework, experiential learning, the skills framework and microcredentials creates an opportunity to establish a common near-curriculum architecture before these elements develop through separate regulatory and administrative routes. It also provides a live window for considering broader sandwich-year access and reusable credit-bearing provision.

Developing the model now allows King’s to pursue structural simplicity from the outset, using a small number of shared frameworks and exploring practical approaches to scheduling, timetabling and administration. This will help the ambition grow through coherent institutional provision rather than accumulated local complexity.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s establishes near-curriculum learning as a recognised part of the formal student experience. Students can pursue learning aligned with their purposes and future ambitions beyond the boundaries of their course, with dedicated space in their studies and appropriate credit or official recognition.

Programmes and faculties can open access to these opportunities through a small number of shared academic, regulatory and administrative structures rather than creating bespoke provision. These structures support adaptable 15-credit modules, interdisciplinary and vertical projects, engagement with external audiences, reflective recognition of experiential learning and broader access to sandwich years.

King’s gains a coherent route through which cross-cutting learning can be governed, assured and provided efficiently. This includes the formal recognition requirements of the skills framework and associated microcredentials, with 2.2.2 supplying the partnership and operational infrastructure needed to support provision.

problemSolved: Higher education lacks a scalable space between the course and extracurricular participation where students can pursue wider ambitions with dedicated time, credit or official recognition.
whatChanges: King’s establishes near-curriculum learning as a recognised part of the formal student experience, giving students dedicated space in their studies to pursue learning aligned with their purposes and future ambitions beyond the boundaries of their course.
